    {
      "chunk": "Links:\n1. [Design a Web Crawler — Sujeet Jaiswal - Principal Software Engineer](https://sujeet.pro/articles/design-web-crawler)\n2. [Web Crawler Architecture: Designing a Scalable Internet Crawler — Codelit.io](https://codelit.io/blog/web-crawler-architecture)\n3. [Design a Distributed Web Crawler | System Design Sandbox](https://www.systemdesignsandbox.com/learn/design-web-crawler)\n4. [Distributed Frontera: Web Crawling At Scale](https://www.zyte.com/blog/distributed-frontera-web-crawling-at-large-scale/)\n5. [Web Crawler System Design: (Step-by-Step Guide)](https://grokkingthesystemdesign.com/guides/web-crawler-system-design/)\n\nSynthesis:\nTo build a production-grade web crawler in 2025, prioritize a distributed, host-partitioned architecture to ensure politeness and scalability [1][2][3].\n\nCore components:\n* URL Frontier: Use a distributed queue (e.g., Kafka) partitioned by host hash [1][2]. This enforces politeness (one host per consumer) and ensures fault tolerance [2][3][4].\n* Deduplication: Use a memory-efficient Bloom filter for URL tracking and SimHash (threshold ≤ 3 bits) for content deduplication [1][5][2].\n* Politeness: Respect RFC 9309 strictly. Cache robots.txt for 24 hours, enforce minimum crawl delays, and implement exponential backoff for 5xx errors [1][2][6].\n* Workers: Keep them stateless and horizontally scalable [5][2][3]. Pre-resolve DNS via local caching (e.g., dnsmasq) to prevent bottlenecks [2][3].\n* Monitoring: Log success rates, response times, and errors. Use proxy rotation and randomized User-Agents to prevent IP-based blocking [7][8].\n\nFor most teams, managed crawling APIs are the most efficient path to production, offering reliable infrastructure and automatic anti-bot handling [9][10]. If building custom, start with a coordinator-worker pattern using Redis for shared state [11].\n\nHighlights:\n\u003cresult id=\"1\"\u003e\n\u003ctitle\u003eDesign a Web Crawler — Sujeet Jaiswal - Principal Software Engineer\u003c/title\u003e\n\u003curl\u003ehttps://sujeet.pro/articles/design-web-crawler\u003c/url\u003e\n\u003ccontent\u003eA web-scale crawler is a distributed system whose hot path is dominated by three concerns: deciding what to fetch next without overloading any single host, recognising URLs and content that have already been seen, and partitioning work so that politeness can be enforced locally. This article walks through a production-shaped design — Kafka-backed coordination, a Mercator-style dual-queue frontier, Bloom + SimHash dedup, and an RFC 9309-compliant robots layer — anchored on the original Mercator and UbiCrawler papers and on what Common Crawl actually publishes month over month.\n[...]\nauthoritative source7\n[...]\n- Parse at least the first 500 KiB of the response (RFC 9309 §2.5).\n[...]\n- Cache for at most 24 h, except when the file is unreachable, in which case the previously cached version may be reused (§2.4).\n[...]\n- Apply longest-match precedence between&#96;Allow&#96; and&#96;Disallow&#96; rules — the rule with the most matching octets wins; on a tie,&#96;Allow&#96; wins (§2.2.2).\n[...]\n- &#96;Crawl-delay&#96; is not in RFC 9309. Bing and Yandex honour it with different semantics; Google explicitly ignores it. Treat it as advisory11.\n[...]\n- URL-level: Bloom filter as the front line for “definitely not seen”, backed by a persistent URL store for the maybe-cases. URL must be normalised first (see URL normalisation).\n- Content-level: 64-bit SimHash fingerprint per page; documents whose fingerprints differ by ≤ 3 bits are treated as near-duplicates. This is the threshold Manku, Jain, and Das Sarma validated empirically on an 8 B-page Google corpus2.\n[...]\n- Deduplicate twice, at different costs. A Bloom filter on URLs (fits in memory, no false negatives) and SimHash on content (catches boilerplate variants) cover the two failure modes — re-fetching the same page, and storing the same page under different URLs.\n[...]\n- Treat RFC 9309 as a hard contract. 500 KiB minimum parse, ≤ 24 h cache, longest-match precedence, no special semantics for&#96;Crawl-delay&#96;. Anything else is folklore.\u003c/content\u003e\n\u003c/result\u003e\n\n\u003cresult id=\"2\"\u003e\n\u003ctitle\u003eWeb Crawler Architecture: Designing a Scalable Internet Crawler — Codelit.io\u003c/title\u003e\n\u003curl\u003ehttps://codelit.io/blog/web-crawler-architecture\u003c/url\u003e\n\u003ccontent\u003eSearch engines, price comparison tools, and AI training pipelines all depend on web crawlers. Designing a crawler that can fetch billions of pages while respecting site policies and handling failures is a foundational system design problem. This guide walks through the complete architecture — from URL frontier to distributed crawling at scale.\n[...]\nThe frontier is far more than a simple queue. It enforces politeness, priority, and freshness.\n[...]\nA single-threaded per-host queue ensures the crawler never hammers one server. The frontier maps each host to its own FIFO queue and enforces a minimum delay between requests to the same host.\n[...]\nBefore crawling any host, fetch and cache its &#96;/robots.txt&#96;. This file specifies:\n[...]\nCache &#96;robots.txt&#96; with a TTL of 24 hours. If the file is unavailable (HTTP 5xx), back off and retry. If it returns 404, assume full access.\n[...]\n(\u003c 1\n[...]\nA single machine can fetch roughly 100–200 pages per second. To crawl billions of pages, distribute the work.\n[...]\nAssign each crawler node a range of hostnames (by consistent hashing). This ensures:\n[...]\nUse a distributed message queue (Kafka) for URL distribution. Discovered URLs are published to a topic, partitioned by host hash. Each crawler node consumes its assigned partitions.\n[...]\n* **HTTP errors** — 4xx errors are permanent (remove URL); 5xx errors are transient (retry with backoff).\n[...]\n* **Timeouts** — retry once, then deprioritize.\n[...]\n* **Spider traps** — detect infinite URL patterns (e.g., calendar pages generating endless dates) by limiting crawl depth per host and detecting repetitive URL structures.\n[...]\n* **Node failure** — the consistent hash ring reassigns partitions to surviving nodes.\n[...]\n* The URL frontier is the brain of the crawler — it manages priority, politeness, and freshness.\n[...]\n* Host-based partitioning simplifies distributed crawling by isolating per-host state.\n[...]\n* JavaScript rendering is expensive — use it selectively.\u003c/content\u003e\n\u003c/result\u003e\n\n\u003cresult id=\"3\"\u003e\n\u003ctitle\u003eDesign a Distributed Web Crawler | System Design Sandbox\u003c/title\u003e\n\u003curl\u003ehttps://www.systemdesignsandbox.com/learn/design-web-crawler\u003c/url\u003e\n\u003ccontent\u003eLearn how to design a production-ready distributed web crawler. This guide covers BFS-based URL frontier, politeness policies with per-host rate limiting, Bloom filters for URL deduplication, custom DNS resolution, distributed worker pools, and scaling to billions of pages.\n[...]\n- Before adding a URL to the frontier, check if it has already been crawled or is already in the queue\n- Uses a Bloom filter for space-efficient membership testing -- 10 billion URLs can be tracked in ~12 GB of memory instead of ~1 TB in a hash set\n- URL normalization removes fragments, resolves relative paths, lowercases the hostname, and sorts query parameters to prevent duplicate entries for the same page\n- Occasional false positives (skipping an uncrawled URL) are acceptable; false negatives (re-crawling the same URL) are not\n[...]\n- A distributed Bloom filter tracking all URLs that have been seen (crawled or enqueued)\n- Before adding a URL to the frontier, check: \"Have we seen this URL?\" If yes, skip. If no, add to both the filter and the frontier.\n- 10 billion URLs at 1% false positive rate = ~12 GB of memory\n- Partitioned across multiple machines by URL hash for distributed operation\n[...]\nBack queues handle politeness. Each domain gets its own queue. A per-domain timer ensures at most 1 request per second (or whatever the domain's&#96;robots.txt&#96; specifies). When a worker requests a URL, the system selects from the back queue whose domain is \"ready\" for another request.\n[...]\nCache&#96;robots.txt&#96; rules in Redis or a local in-memory cache. Refresh periodically (e.g., every 24 hours) since sites update their rules.\n[...]\nBetter: Enforce per-domain rate limits (typically 1 request per second). Use back queues partitioned by domain with per-domain timers. Respect&#96;robots.txt&#96; crawl-delay directives. A polite crawler that runs for months is far more effective than an aggressive crawler that gets blocked in hours. See rate limiting algorithms for implementation strategies.\n[...]\nDon't just say\u003c/content\u003e\n\u003c/result\u003e\n\n\u003cresult id=\"4\"\u003e\n\u003ctitle\u003eDistributed Frontera: Web Crawling At Scale\u003c/title\u003e\n\u003curl\u003ehttps://www.zyte.com/blog/distributed-frontera-web-crawling-at-large-scale/\u003c/url\u003e\n\u003ccontent\u003eWe began investigating how to scale this existing solution and make it work with an arbitrary number of documents. Considering our access pattern, we were interested in scalable key-value storage that could offer random read/writes and efficient batch processing capabilities.\n[...]\nas a communication layer.\n[...]\nLet’s start with the spiders. The seed URLs are propagated to strategy workers and DataBase (DB) workers by means of a Kafka topic named 'Spider Log'. Strategy workers decide which pages to crawl using the document states stored in HBase. The workers then assign a score to each page and send the results to the 'Scoring Log' topic.\n[...]\nA DB worker stores all kinds of metadata, including content and scores. The DB worker checks the consumer offset of the spiders, generating new batches if needed and sending them to the ‘New Batches’ topic. Spiders consume these batches, downloading each page and extracting links from them. The links are then sent to the ‘Spider Log’ topic where they are stored and scored. And that's it, we have a closed circle.\n[...]\nThe main advantage of this design is real-time operation. The crawling strategy can be changed without having to stop the crawl. It’s worth mentioning that the crawling strategy can be implemented as a separate module. This module would contain the logic for ordering URLs and stopping crawls, as well as the scoring model.\n[...]\nOne spider thread can crawl around 1200 pages/minute from 100 web hosts in parallel. The spider to worker ratio is about 4:1 without content; storing content could require more workers.\n[...]\n1 GB of memory is required for each strategy worker instance in order to run the state cache. The size of the state cache can be accommodated through a settings adjustment. For example, if you need to crawl 15K pages/minute, you would need 12 spiders and 3 pairs of strategy workers and DB workers. All these processes would consume 18 cores in total.\u003c/content\u003e\n\u003c/result\u003e\n\n\u003cresult id=\"5\"\u003e\n\u003ctitle\u003eWeb Crawler System Design: (Step-by-Step Guide)\u003c/title\u003e\n\u003curl\u003ehttps://grokkingthesystemdesign.com/guides/web-crawler-system-design/\u003c/url\u003e\n\u003ccontent\u003eThe crawler must also apply duplicate detection since duplicate pages are pervasive on the web. This includes detecting duplicate URLs through normalization, duplicate content through cryptographic hashing, and near-duplicate pages through SimHash, MinHash, and content shingling algorithms. Respecting robots.txt and politeness policies ensures the crawler behaves responsibly by obeying robots.txt rules, following crawl-delay directives, and throttling requests so individual domains are not overloaded.\n[...]\n100,\n[...]\nClean URLs that survive deduplication re-enter the frontier with updated priority scores based on source page importance, link depth, and freshness requirements. Raw and processed data flows to\n[...]\nA responsible crawler must follow robots.txt rules that specify which paths can be crawled and by which user agents. The robots.txt file may also specify crawl-delay directives indicating minimum intervals between requests. Even without explicit directives, throttling requests based on observed server response times prevents overwhelming smaller websites that cannot handle aggressive crawling. A server responding slowly is effectively signaling that it needs relief, and a well-designed crawler should back off automatically rather than adding to the load.\n[...]\nDomain-level rate limiting requires maintaining state about recent requests to each domain, including timestamps and response characteristics. This state must be accessible to all workers that might crawl that domain, typically handled through a shared cache like Redis or a distributed rate limiting service. The rate limiter should adapt to server behavior dynamically. It should back off when response times increase or error rates rise, and potentially increase throughput when servers respond quickly and consistently. This adaptive approach maximizes crawl efficiency while maintaining politeness across varying server capabilities.\n[...]\n500TB monthly, making compression essential for cost control.\u003c/content\u003e\n\u003c/result\u003e",

[Robots.txt](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Robots.txt)\n\nSynthesis:\nRFC 9309 defines the standard for robots.txt crawlers [1][2]. Requirements include:\n\nAccess and Retrieval\nFiles must be at /robots.txt on the root path of the service [1][3]. Crawlers must follow at least five consecutive redirects [1][3]. The file must be UTF-8 encoded and text/plain [1][3].\n\nParsing and Matching\nCrawlers must support user-agent case-insensitive matching [1][3]. If multiple groups match, their rules are combined [1][3]. Path matching is case-sensitive, using the longest (most specific) match [1][3][4]. If Allow and Disallow rules are equivalent in length, Allow takes precedence [1][3]. \n\nLimits and Caching\nImplementations must process at least 500 KiB [3][5]. Caching is permitted but should not exceed 24 hours unless the file is unreachable [1][3]. \n\nErrors\n4xx status codes are treated as \"unavailable\" (all allowed), while 5xx codes are \"unreachable\" (all disallowed) [4][2].\n\nHighlights:\n\u003cresult id=\"1\"\u003e\n\u003ctitle\u003eRFC 9309 - Robots Exclusion Protocol\u003c/title\u003e\n\u003curl\u003ehttps://datatracker.ietf.org/doc/html/rfc9309.html\u003c/url\u003e\n\u003ccontent\u003eThis document specifies and extends the \"Robots Exclusion Protocol\" method\n[...]\nThe protocol language consists of rule(s) and group(s) that the service makes available in a file named \"robots.txt\" as described in Section 2.3:¶\n[...]\nRule: A line with a key-value pair that defines how a crawler may access URIs. If an \"allow\" rule and a \"disallow\" rule are equivalent, then the \"allow\" rule SHOULD be used. If no match is found amongst the rules in a group for a matching user-agent or there are no rules in the group, the URI is allowed. In September 2022, the Robots Exclusion Protocol behind robots.txt was published as an IETF Proposed Standard, RFC 9309. It formalizes syntax, parsing, caching rules, and clarifies that robots.txt is advisory guidance, not an access-control mechanism.\n[...]\n- Formal definition of syntax for&#96;robots.txt&#96;(plain text, UTF-8, line breaks CR/CRLF/LF) and exact location (root directory, same protocol/host/port) for the file.\n- Formal grammar for&#96;User-agent&#96;,&#96;Allow&#96;, and&#96;Disallow&#96;, including how groups are defined and terminated.\n[...]\n- Rules for handling errors (HTTP status codes, caching the file) so crawlers know how to behave when&#96;robots.txt&#96; is unreachable.\n[...]\n- Definition of which directives are standardized, what rules crawlers must support for net interoperability.\n[...]\n- Error handling, 4xx makes the file “unavailable” which permits crawling, 5xx makes it “unreachable” which requires crawlers to assume a full disallow until conditions clear.\n[...]\n- Specification notes that the protocol is advisory only (not an access authorization mechanism).\n- Redirect handling, crawlers should follow up to five chained redirects for robots.txt.\n- Caching guidance, cache robots.txt, but do not rely on a cached copy longer than 24 hours unless the file is unreachable.\n[...]\n94 people)\n[...]\nrules for bots\n[...]\n, missing files and foo\n[...]\nThe IETF formally publishes RFC 930\n[...]\n, titled Robots Exclusion Protocol. This gives robots.txt an official place\n[...]\nthe standards catalog for the first time since\n[...]\n1994!\n[...]\n- You now have an authoritative reference when diagnosing crawl problems\n- Line parsing rules are no longer social meme tribal knowledge\n- Redirect chains, file size quirks, error issues, and failures now have required defined behavior\n- Crawler authors have no excuse for inconsistent or ambiguous handling of crawling\n- Tools like validators and site auditors can benchmark behavior directly against the RFC 9309\u003c/content\u003e\n\u003c/result\u003e\n\n\u003cresult id=\"3\"\u003e\n\u003ctitle\u003eRfc9309\u003c/title\u003e\n\u003curl\u003ehttps://www.rfc-editor.org/rfc/rfc9309.txt\u003c/url\u003e\n\u003ccontent\u003eThe protocol language consists of rule(s) and group(s) that the\n service makes available in a file named \"robots.txt\" as described in\n Section 2.3:\n\n Rule: A line with a key-value pair that defines how a crawler may\n access URIs. The last group may have no rules, which\n means it implicitly allows everything.\n[...]\na product token\n[...]\nRFC9110\n[...]\nproduct token SHOULD be a substring in\n the User-Agent\n[...]\nCrawlers MUST use case-insensitive matching to find the group that\n matches the product token and then obey the rules of the group. If\n there is more than one group matching the user-agent, the matching\n groups\n[...]\nrules MUST be\n[...]\ninto one group\n[...]\nTo evaluate if access to a URI is allowed, a crawler MUST match the\n paths in \"allow\" and \"disallow\" rules against the URI. The matching\n SHOULD be case sensitive. The matching MUST start with the first\n octet of the path. The most specific match found MUST be used. The\n most specific match is the match that has the most octets. Duplicate\n rules in a group MAY be deduplicated. If an \"allow\" rule and a\n \"disallow\" rule are equivalent, then the \"allow\" rule SHOULD be used.\n If no match is found amongst the rules in a group for a matching\n user-agent or there are no rules in the group, the URI is allowed.\n The /robots.txt URI is implicitly allowed.\n\n Octets\n[...]\nThe rules MUST be accessible in a file named \"/robots.txt\" (all\n lowercase) in the top-level path of the service. The file MUST be\n UTF-8 encoded (as defined in [RFC3629]) and Internet Media Type\n \"text/plain\" (as defined in [RFC2046]).\n[...]\nCrawlers MUST try to parse each line of the robots.txt file.\n Crawlers MUST use the parseable rules.\n[...]\nCrawlers MAY\n[...]\nthe fetched robots.\n[...]\nfile is unreachable.\u003c/content\u003e\n\u003c/result\u003e\n\n\u003cresult id=\"4\"\u003e\n\u003ctitle\u003eRespecting Robots Exclusion Protocol or robots.txt at Scale | by Rashad Moarref | GumGum Tech Blog | Medium\u003c/title\u003e\n\u003curl\u003ehttps://medium.com/gumgum-tech/respecting-robots-exclusion-protocol-or-robots-txt-at-scale-60ee57dc1295\u003c/url\u003e\n\u003ccontent\u003eTo interpret the robots.txt file, we follow the guidelines set forth in RFC 9309, which standardizes how web crawlers should process the file’s directives. The key criteria are as follows:\n[...]\n- If both&#96;Allow&#96; and&#96;Disallow&#96; rules match the same URL, the longest (most specific) rule takes precedence.\n- Rules are applied based on the most specific match, ensuring that detailed patterns override broader ones.\n- URLs and path matching are case-sensitive, meaning&#96;/path&#96; and&#96;/Path&#96; are treated as distinct URLs.\n[...]\n- The&#96;*&#96; wildcard can be used to match any sequence of characters in a URL.\n- The&#96;$&#96; symbol is used to match the end of a URL, allowing for precise control over which resources can be crawled.\n- Special characters within the URL, such as&#96;%&#96;, must be handled as they are represented in the URL (percent-encoded).\n[...]\n- Robots.txt files should be cached for a reasonable amount of time, typically recommended as up to 24 hours, to minimize unnecessary retrieval from the server.\n- If a server returns a 5xx error (server-side error), the crawler should assume&#96;Disallow&#96;, meaning no crawling should occur.\n- For 4xx errors (client-side errors), the crawler may assume&#96;Allow&#96;, meaning the site can be crawled, as this typically indicates the robots.txt file or requested resource is missing but the server is functional.\n[...]\nrobots.txt&#96; file\n[...]\nIn this blog post, we provide a patch inspired by this article, that enhances Python’s&#96;urllib.robotparser&#96; functionality to conform to RFC 9309. Specifically, it handles&#96;*&#96; wildcard and&#96;$&#96; end-of-path symbols. It returns the allowance for the most specific path when there is a conflict between&#96;Allow&#96; and&#96;Disallow&#96; rules and defaults to&#96;Allow&#96; when both rules have the same path specificity. Additionally, it manages edge cases that could break regex parsing, such as rules containing multiple repeated wildcards like&#96;******&#96; or intricate regular expressions like&#96;.*.*.*.*&#96;. The full implementation is as follows:\u003c/content\u003e\n\u003c/result\u003e\n\n\u003cresult id=\"5\"\u003e\n\u003ctitle\u003eRobots.txt\u003c/title\u003e\n\u003curl\u003ehttps://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Robots.txt\u003c/url\u003e\n\u003ccontent\u003eOn July 1, 2019, Google announced the proposal of the Robots Exclusion Protocol as an official standard under Internet Engineering Task Force. A proposed standard was published in September 2022 as RFC 9309.\n[...]\nA site owner wishing to give instructions to web robots places a text file called robots.txt in the root of the web site hierarchy (e.g. https://www.example.com/robots.txt). This text file contains the instructions in a specific format (see examples below). Robots that choose to follow the instructions try to fetch this file and read the instructions before fetching any other file from the website. If this file does not exist, web robots assume that the website owner does not wish to place any limitations on crawling the entire site.\n[...]\nA robots.txt\n[...]\norigin. For websites with\n[...]\ndomains, each subdomain\n[...]\ntxt file. If example\n[...]\ndid not, the rules that would apply\n[...]\ncom would not apply to foo\n[...]\nexample.com. In\n[...]\neach URI scheme and port\n[...]\nrobots.txt\n[...]\n; http://example.com\n[...]\ntxt does not apply to pages\n[...]\nDespite the use of the terms allow and disallow, the protocol is purely advisory and\n[...]\nweb robot; it cannot enforce any of what is stated\n[...]\nthe file. Malicious web robots are unlikely to honor robots.txt; some may even use the robots.txt as a guide to find disallowed links and go straight to them. While this is sometimes claimed to be a security risk, this\n[...]\nof security through obscurity is discouraged by standards bodies. The National Institute of Standards and Technology(NIST) in the United States\n[...]\nSystem security should not depend on the secrecy of the implementation or its components.\" In the\n[...]\nfiles, security through obscurity is not recommended as a security technique.\n[...]\nThe Robots Exclusion Protocol requires crawlers to parse at least 500 kibibytes (512000 bytes) of robots.txt files, which Google maintains as a 500 kibibyte file size restriction for robots.txt files.\u003c/content\u003e\n\u003c/result\u003e",

```json
{
  "isFinal": true,
  "references": [
    {
      "chunk": "Links:\n1. [Normalization](https://www.boost.org/doc/libs/1_84_0/libs/url/doc/html/url/urls/normalization.html)\n2. [URI normalization](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/URL_normalization)\n3. [De-duping URLs via rewrite rules](https://dl.acm.org/doi/10.1145/1401890.1401917)\n4. [URL Normalization for De-duplication of Web Pages](https://www.cs.cornell.edu/~hema/papers/sp0955-agarwalATS.pdf)\n5. [Learning Center - CanonicalCheck Pro](https://canonicalcheck.io/learn)\n\nSynthesis:\nURL normalization transforms syntactically different URLs into a single canonical form to identify equivalent resources [1][2][3]. Crawlers use this process to avoid fetching duplicate content, which saves bandwidth and preserves crawl budget [4][2][5].\n\nStandard techniques include case normalization, percent-encoding resolution, and removing default ports or path segments [1][6]. Advanced approaches involve mining crawl logs to create rewrite rules or using machine learning to generalize patterns, allowing crawlers to handle unseen URLs without fetching their content [4][3][7].\n\nHighlights:\n\u003cresult id=\"1\"\u003e\n\u003ctitle\u003eNormalization\u003c/title\u003e\n\u003curl\u003ehttps://www.boost.org/doc/libs/1_84_0/libs/url/doc/html/url/urls/normalization.html\u003c/url\u003e\n\u003ccontent\u003eNormalization allows us to determine if two URLs refer to the same resource. URLs comparisons serve the same purpose, where two strings are compared as if they were normalized.\n[...]\nThere is no way to determine whether two URLs refer to the same resource without full knowledge or control of them. Thus, equivalence is based on string comparisons augmented by additional URL and scheme rules. This means comparison is not sufficient to determine whether two URLs identify different resources as the same resource can always be served from different addresses.\n[...]\nFor this reason, comparison methods are designed to minimize false negatives while strictly avoiding false positives. In other words, if two URLs compare equal, they definitely represent the same resource. If they are considered different, they might still refer to the same resource depending on the application.\n[...]\nBy only considering the rules of rfc3986, Simple String Comparison fails to identify the URLs above point to the same resource. The comparison operators implement Syntax-Based Normalization, which implements the rules defined by rfc3986.\n[...]\nIn mutable URLs, the member function normalize can used to be apply Syntax-Based Normalization to a URL. A normalized URL is represented by a canonical string where any two strings that would compare equal end up with the same underlying representation. In other words, Simple String Comparison of two normalized URLs is equivalent to Syntax-Based Normalization.\n[...]\nNormalization uses the following definitions of rfc3986 to minimize false negatives:\n[...]\nSyntax-Based Normalization can also be used as a first step for Scheme-Based and Protocol-Based Normalization. One common scheme-specific rule is ignoring the default port for that scheme and empty absolute paths:\n[...]\nOther criteria commonly used to minimize false negatives for specific schemes are:\u003c/content\u003e\n\u003c/result\u003e\n\n\u003cresult id=\"2\"\u003e\n\u003ctitle\u003eURI normalization\u003c/title\u003e\n\u003curl\u003ehttps://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/URL_normalization\u003c/url\u003e\n\u003ccontent\u003eURI normalization is the process by which URIs are modified and standardized in a consistent manner. The goal of the normalization process is to transform a URI into a normalized URI so it is possible to determine if two syntactically different URIs may be equivalent.\n[...]\nSearch engines employ URI normalization in order to correctly rank pages that may be found with multiple URIs, and to reduce indexing of duplicate pages. Web crawlers perform URI normalization in order to avoid crawling the same resource more than once. Web browsers may perform normalization to determine if a link has been visited or to determine if a page has been cached. Web servers may also perform normalization for many reasons (i.e. to be able to more easily intercept security risks coming from client requests, to use only one absolute file name for each resource stored in their caches, named in log files, etc.).\n[...]\n- Removing duplicate slashes Paths which include two adjacent slashes could be converted to one. Example:\n[...]\nSome normalization rules may be developed for specific websites by examining URI lists obtained from previous crawls or web server logs. For example, if the URI\n[...]\nin a crawl log several times along\n[...]\nwe may assume that the two URIs are equivalent and can be normalized to one of the URI forms.\n[...]\nSchonfeld et al. (2006) present a heuristic called DustBuster for detecting DUST (different URIs with similar text) rules that can be applied to URI lists. They showed that once the correct DUST rules were found and applied with a normalization algorithm, they were able to find up to 68% of the redundant URIs in a URI list.\n[...]\n- URL(Uniform Resource Locator)\n- URI fragment\n- Web crawler\u003c/content\u003e\n\u003c/result\u003e\n\n\u003cresult id=\"3\"\u003e\n\u003ctitle\u003eDe-duping URLs via rewrite rules\u003c/title\u003e\n\u003curl\u003ehttps://dl.acm.org/doi/10.1145/1401890.1401917\u003c/url\u003e\n\u003ccontent\u003eA large fraction of the URLs on the web contain duplicate (or near-duplicate) content. De-duping URLs is an extremely important problem for search engines, since all the principal functions of a search engine, including crawling, indexing, ranking, and presentation, are adversely impacted by the presence of duplicate URLs. Traditionally, the de-duping problem has been addressed by fetching and examining the content of the URL; our approach here is different. Given a set of URLs partitioned into equivalence classes based on the content (URLs in the same equivalence class have similar content), we address the problem of mining this set and learning URL rewrite rules that transform all URLs of an equivalence class to the same canonical form. These rewrite rules can then be applied to eliminate duplicates among URLs that are encountered for the first time during crawling, even*without*fetching their content.\n[...]\nIn order to express such transformation rules, we propose a simple framework that is general enough to capture the most common URL rewrite patterns occurring on the web; in particular, it encapsulates the DUST (*D*ifferent*U*RLs with similar*t*ext) framework [5]. We provide an efficient algorithm for mining and learning URL rewrite rules and show that under mild assumptions, it is complete, i.e., our algorithm learns every URL rewrite rule that is correct, for an appropriate notion of correctness. We demonstrate the expressiveness of our framework and the effectiveness of our algorithm by performing a variety of extensive large-scale experiments.\n[...]\nDuplicate URLs have brought serious troubles to the whole pipeline of a search engine, from crawling, indexing, to result serving. URL normalization is to transform duplicate URLs to a canonical form using a set of rewrite rules. Nowadays URL ...\n[...]\naliases and redire\n[...]\ngenerates the same page from various different URL requests.\u003c/content\u003e\n\u003c/result\u003e\n\n\u003cresult id=\"4\"\u003e\n\u003ctitle\u003eURL Normalization for De-duplication of Web Pages\u003c/title\u003e\n\u003curl\u003ehttps://www.cs.cornell.edu/~hema/papers/sp0955-agarwalATS.pdf\u003c/url\u003e\n\u003ccontent\u003eABSTRACT Presence of duplicate documents in the World Wide Web ad versely affects crawling, indexing and relevance, which are the core building blocks of web search. In this paper, we present a set of techniques to mine rules from URLs and utilize these learnt rules for de-duplication using just URL strings without fetching the content explicitly. Our tech nique is composed of mining the crawl logs and utilizing clusters of similar pages to extract specific rules from URLs belonging to each cluster. Preserving each mined rules for de-duplication is not efficient due to the large number of specific rules. We present a machine learning technique to generalize the set of rules, which reduces the resource foot print to be usable at web-scale. The rule extraction tech niques are robust against web-site specific URL conventions. We demonstrate the effectiveness of our techniques through experimental evaluation.\n[...]\n3.2.1\n[...]\nthe dup-\n[...]\n5. CONCLUSIONS In this paper we presented a set of scalable and robust techniques for de-duplication of URLs. Our techniques are scalable to Web due to feasible computational complexity. We presented basic and deep tokenization of URLs to ex tract all possible tokens from URLs which are mined by our Rule generation techniques for generating normaliza tion Rules. We presented a novel Rule generation technique which uses efficient ranking methodologies for reducing the number of pair-wise Rules. Pair-wise Rules thus generated are consumed by decision tree algorithm to generate pre cise generalized Rules. We evaluated the effectiveness of our techniques on 7.78M URL data set. Our results show that we perform significantly well on key metrics.\u003c/content\u003e\n\u003c/result\u003e\n\n\u003cresult id=\"5\"\u003e\n\u003ctitle\u003eLearning Center - CanonicalCheck Pro\u003c/title\u003e\n\u003curl\u003ehttps://canonicalcheck.io/learn\u003c/url\u003e\n\u003ccontent\u003etell search engines which\n[...]\nis the \"master\n[...]\nURL normalization is the process of ensuring all URL variations of the same page redirect to a single, consistent URL format.\n[...]\n- Avoid Content Duplication: Multiple URLs serving identical content confuse search engines\n- Consolidate Link Equity: External links may point to different variations, splitting authority\n- Improve Analytics: Traffic is scattered across URL variations, making reporting difficult\n- Better User Experience: Consistent URLs are easier to remember and share\n[...]\n: Redirect all non-\n[...]\nto your chosen format\n[...]\nUpdate Internal Links: Ensure all\n[...]\nuse the standard\n[...]\n. Set Canonical Tags: Reinforce your choice with proper canonical tags\n[...]\nConfigure Sitemaps: List only the canonical versions in your XML sitemap\n[...]\nTest All Variations: Use our URL\n[...]\n- Redirect Chains: Each hop in a chain consumes crawl budget without reaching content\n- Duplicate URLs: Crawlers waste time indexing the same content from different URLs\n- Error Pages: Broken redirects or chains leading to 404s waste crawl resources\n[...]\n1. Eliminate Chains: Direct all redirects to final destinations in one hop\n2. Normalize URLs: Ensure only one URL version per page is crawlable\n3. Use Canonical Tags: Signal preferred URLs to reduce duplicate crawling\n4. Monitor Server Logs: Track how search engines crawl your site\n5. Regular Audits: Use our tools to identify and fix issues before they impact rankings\n[...]\n- ✓Redirect Chains: No URL should redirect more than once to reach its destination\n- ✓Canonical Tags: Every page has one, they're consistent, and they don't conflict\n[...]\n- ✓URL Normalization: All variations (http/https, www/non-www) redirect to one standard\n[...]\n- ✓Trailing Slashes: Consistent policy across the site\n[...]\n- ✓Internal Links: Updated to point to canonical URLs, avoiding redirects\n[...]\nRedirect Chains After\n[...]\nto non-\u003c/content\u003e\n\u003c/result\u003e",

[Robots.txt for SEO: Complete Guide + AI Crawlers | Similarweb](https://www.similarweb.com/blog/marketing/seo/robots-txt/)\n\nSynthesis:\nEthical web crawling requires acting predictably and responsibly to avoid server harm. \n\nRespect robots.txt by reading it before crawling, specifically obeying the User-agent, Disallow, and Crawl-delay directives. Note that robots.txt is a courtesy, not a legal shield; it does not replace the need for data collection policies or compliance with copyright laws.\n\nImplement proactive rate limiting—such as a token bucket—to stay below a site's capacity. When you receive an HTTP 429 error, use exponential backoff with jitter to retry. Always prioritize the Retry-After header if provided. Avoid spider traps by identifying and steering clear of recursive or dynamically generated URL loops. Finally, always provide a transparent User-Agent string with contact information so administrators can reach you.\n\nHighlights:\n\u003cresult id=\"1\"\u003e\n\u003ctitle\u003eEthical Web Scraping: Rate Limiting \u0026 robots.txt Guide (2026) - SparkProxy\u003c/title\u003e\n\u003curl\u003ehttps://www.sparkproxy.io/blog/guide-on-ethical-scraping-and-rate-limiting\u003c/url\u003e\n\u003ccontent\u003e- A&#96;Crawl-delay&#96; directive in&#96;robots.txt&#96; is a binding request, not a suggestion. Ignoring it is the fastest path to IP bans and potential legal action.\n[...]\n- Rate limiting and respectful crawling protect your scraper's long-term viability as much as they protect the servers you depend on.\n[...]\nEthical web scraping means collecting publicly accessible data in a way that respects the server owner's stated preferences, avoids measurable performance degradation, and complies with applicable law. The term maps to a concrete set of behaviours: reading&#96;robots.txt&#96; before sending a single request, introducing deliberate delays between requests, identifying your bot transparently, and collecting only the data you actually need.\n[...]\nThe robots exclusion protocol has been a web standard since 1994. RFC 9309 (published 2022) formalised it. The three fields your scraper must respect are&#96;User-agent&#96;,&#96;Disallow&#96;, and&#96;Crawl-delay&#96;.\n[...]\nThe crawl-delay directive is the most violated rule in practical scraping. Sites that detect a&#96;robots.txt&#96; fetch followed by immediate disregard for&#96;Crawl-delay&#96; treat this as a stronger signal of bad-faith behaviour than sites that never fetched&#96;robots.txt&#96; at all. It's a detectable pattern, and it escalates bot-detection responses.\n[...]\nAccording to a\n[...]\nRate-based causes of action. Even when scraping is lawful, causing measurable server performance degradation can expose you to CFAA \"damage\" claims or civil trespass-to-chattels doctrine. Proper rate limiting removes this risk entirely. A scraper respecting&#96;Crawl-delay&#96; directives and responding correctly to 429s has never been found liable under any of these theories.\n[...]\nIf&#96;robots.txt&#96; specifies a&#96;Crawl-delay&#96;, use exactly that value. Without a directive, 1 request per 1–2 seconds is safe for most production sites. For small community-run sites, drop to 1 request per 5 seconds. The right number is the one where your scraper causes no measurable latency increase for real users.\u003c/content\u003e\n\u003c/result\u003e\n\n\u003cresult id=\"2\"\u003e\n\u003ctitle\u003eResponsible Web Scraping: Rate Limiting, robots.txt, and Keeping Your Scraper Legal - DEV Community\u003c/title\u003e\n\u003curl\u003ehttps://dev.to/scraping_eng/responsible-web-scraping-rate-limiting-robotstxt-and-keeping-your-scraper-legal-55n2\u003c/url\u003e\n\u003ccontent\u003eYou can build a scraper that's technically brilliant — stealth patches, CAPTCHA solving, proxy rotation. But if you ignore rate limits, blast servers with requests, and violate terms of service, you're not a developer. You're a problem.\n[...]\n1. Legal risk — lawsuits are real (hiQ v. LinkedIn, Clearview AI)\n2. IP bans — aggressive scraping gets you permanently blocked\n3. Server harm — you can accidentally DDoS small sites\n4. Reputation — your company's IP range gets blacklisted\n5. Data quality — rushed scraping produces worse data\n[...]\nEvery website can publish a&#96;robots.txt\\&#96; file that specifies which paths scrapers should avoid:\n[...]\nrobots.txt is advisory, not legally binding in most jurisdictions. But:\n[...]\n- Follow it for sites you have no business relationship with\n- Respect Crawl-delay — it's telling you their server's capacity\n- Document your decisions — if you choose to ignore specific rules, have a reason\n[...]\nLimiting: Don't Be That\n[...]\n### Adaptive Rate Limiting\n[...]\n- [ ] robots.txt — Are you checking and respecting it?\n- [ ] Rate limiting — Are you waiting between requests?\n- [ ] User-Agent — Does it identify your bot and provide contact info?\n- [ ] Caching — Are you avoiding re-scraping unchanged pages?\n- [ ] Error handling — Do you back off on 429/5xx responses?\n- [ ] CAPTCHA budget — Have you set a daily spending limit?\n- [ ] Data storage — Are you only keeping data you actually need?\n- [ ] Terms of Service — Have you read the site's ToS?\n[...]\n1. robots.txt is your first check — respect it unless you have a documented reason not to\n2. Adaptive rate limiting is better than fixed delays — respond to server signals\n3. Cache aggressively — don't re-scrape what hasn't changed\n4. Budget your CAPTCHA solves — set daily limits and stick to them\n5. Identify yourself — a clear User-Agent helps everyone\n6. Slow is reliable — a scraper that runs for a week at 1 req/s beats one that gets banned in an hour\u003c/content\u003e\n\u003c/result\u003e\n\n\u003cresult id=\"3\"\u003e\n\u003ctitle\u003eAvoid Scraping Lawsuits: Technical Controls Guide\u003c/title\u003e\n\u003curl\u003ehttps://containers.news/from-crawlers-to-courtrooms-technical-controls-to-avoid-scra\u003c/url\u003e\n\u003ccontent\u003eThis guide focuses on the engineering controls that reduce exposure: rate limits, robots.txt respect, provenance metadata, differential privacy, opt-out mechanisms, and the documentation needed for legal defense. If you have ever built reproducible analytics pipelines, you already understand the value of deterministic inputs and auditability. The same thinking applies here: your crawler should behave like a well-governed production service, not a one-off script that leaves no evidence behind. In many cases, the difference between a defensible collection practice and a lawsuit is whether you can show intent, controls, and escalation handling.\n[...]\nRate limiting is often described as a stability feature, but it is also a fairness and risk-reduction mechanism. A crawler that respects per-domain QPS, per-path burst limits, and backoff on error codes looks materially different from one that fans out aggressively. Implement both client-side pacing and server-response-aware backoff, including 429, 503, and unusually high timeout rates. Keep your limits conservative enough that a single configuration error does not result in a rapid flood of requests.\n[...]\nteams know where\n[...]\nAt runtime, enforce rate limits, backoff, blocklists, content filtering, and alerting on anomalies. If the crawler starts seeing unusual error spikes, new redirects, or source policy changes, stop and review. Treat exceptions as events, not as things to route around. This mentality is very close to the incident discipline needed in AI-driven user systems, where small signals can indicate major downstream risk.\n[...]\nNo. Legality depends on the source, the method, the data collected, the purpose, and the applicable contracts or laws. A restrained system that respects robots.txt, rate limits, and opt-outs is materially different from an indiscriminate crawler that bypasses boundaries. The safest approach is to treat each source as a governed relationship rather than a generic fetch target.\u003c/content\u003e\n\u003c/result\u003e\n\n\u003cresult id=\"4\"\u003e\n\u003ctitle\u003eRobots.txt Is Not Enough Anymore: What Developers Need to Know About AI Crawler Controls - DEV Community\u003c/title\u003e\n\u003curl\u003ehttps://dev.to/promptcloud_services/robotstxt-is-not-enough-anymore-what-developers-need-to-know-about-ai-crawler-controls-3cj0\u003c/url\u003e\n\u003ccontent\u003e*What robots.txt actually does * Robots.txt is a convention for communicating crawler preferences. It lets website owners specify which user agents should avoid which paths. Google’s own documentation describes it mainly as a way to manage crawler traffic, and it also makes an important point: robots.txt does not enforce crawler behavior. A crawler has to choose to obey it. If the goal is to keep information secure, stronger access controls are needed.\n[...]\nCloudflare introduced tools that help website owners control whether AI bots are allowed to access content for model training, including managed robots.txt support and options to block AI bots from ad-monetized portions of a site. Cloudflare also introduced Pay Per Crawl, which lets publishers choose whether to allow, charge, or block a crawler.\n[...]\nCloudflare’s\n[...]\nsystems, for example,\n[...]\n*What developers should build differently * The first practical change is to stop treating robots.txt as a one-time check. It should be part of a broader source policy layer.\n[...]\nA crawler system should record the robots.txt state it observed, when it observed it, and how that affected crawl decisions. If the source later changes its policy, teams need to know which datasets were collected before and after that change.\n[...]\nThe second change is crawler identity discipline. Crawlers should identify themselves clearly, consistently, and responsibly. They should not rely on misleading user agents or behavior that creates ambiguity.\n[...]\nThe third change is policy-aware scheduling. If a source has crawl-delay expectations, AI-specific restrictions, or access conditions, scheduling logic should reflect that. Source policy should influence crawl behavior.\n[...]\nRobots.txt remains one signal. Crawler identity, AI-specific user agents, licensing signals, edge enforcement, provenance, and policy-aware scheduling are becoming part of the same stack.\u003c/content\u003e\n\u003c/result\u003e\n\n\u003cresult id=\"5\"\u003e\n\u003ctitle\u003eRobots.txt for SEO: Complete Guide + AI Crawlers | Similarweb\u003c/title\u003e\n\u003curl\u003ehttps://www.similarweb.com/blog/marketing/seo/robots-txt/\u003c/url\u003e\n\u003ccontent\u003eBut there’s a newer dimension to\n[...]\nconversation. Since 2023, a fleet of AI crawlers – GPTBot, ClaudeBot, PerplexityBot, and others – has joined the traditional search bots hitting your site.\n[...]\nbots follow the same robots.txt rules, and how you handle them is now a real business decision: some sites\n[...]\nthem to protect proprietary content, while others make sure they’re not accidentally keeping\n[...]\ncrawlers out.\n[...]\nnuance: robots.\n[...]\ngenerally follow, but they\n[...]\nre not a guarantee.\n[...]\nare not supported in robots.\n[...]\nFor controlling indexing, use the meta noindex tag or the X-Robots-Tag HTTP header instead. For controlling crawl rate, Google deprecated its Search Console crawl rate limiter tool in January 2024. The current recommended approach is to use server response codes(such as 429 or 503) to signal to Googlebot that it should slow down.\n[...]\nReputable crawlers from Google, Bing, OpenAI, Anthropic, and Perplexity treat robots.txt as binding. However, robots.txt is a voluntary standard with no technical enforcement mechanism – a bot can read your file and ignore it entirely. Malicious scrapers and some aggressive AI crawlers have been documented doing exactly this. For bots, you need to actually stop rather than just instruct, server-level blocking (via a WAF, CDN rules, or IP blocking) is more reliable than robots.txt alone.\n[...]\n, not whether\n[...]\n. A page\n[...]\nuse a&#96;no\n[...]\nIt depends on your goals. Blocking training crawlers (GPTBot, ClaudeBot, Google-Extended) protects your content from being used to train AI models without attribution. Allowing retrieval crawlers (OAI-SearchBot, Claude-SearchBot, PerplexityBot) keeps your content eligible to appear in AI answers. The two types of bots are independent – you can block one category without affecting\u003c/content\u003e\n\u003c/result\u003e",

[closed]](https://stackoverflow.com/questions/17872753/what-is-the-difference-between-scrapys-spider-middleware-and-downloader-middlew)\n\nSynthesis:\nScrapy is an event-driven, asynchronous framework for web scraping that uses a central Engine to coordinate data flow between components [1][2]. The Engine manages the Scheduler for pending requests, the Downloader for fetching web pages, Spiders for parsing data, and Item Pipelines for processing results [1][3].\n\nFor middleware, use Downloader Middleware to globally alter outgoing requests or incoming responses, such as for proxy rotation, user-agent management, or caching [4][5]. Use Spider Middleware to post-process items, requests, or exceptions specifically as they move between the Engine and your Spider [5][6]. Always assign explicit numerical orders to your middleware to control execution sequence, as Scrapy processes them in increasing order for requests and decreasing order for responses [4][7][6].\n\nHighlights:\n\u003cresult id=\"1\"\u003e\n\u003ctitle\u003eArchitecture overview — Scrapy 2.15.1 documentation\u003c/title\u003e\n\u003curl\u003ehttps://docs.scrapy.org/en/stable/topics/architecture.html\u003c/url\u003e\n\u003ccontent\u003ebelow with links for more\n[...]\n4. The Engine sends the Requests to the Downloader, passing through the Downloader Middlewares (see process_request()).\n[...]\n5. Once the page finishes downloading the Downloader generates a Response (with that page) and sends it to the Engine, passing through the Downloader Middlewares (see process_response()).\n[...]\n6. The Engine receives the Response from the Downloader and sends it to the Spider for processing, passing through the Spider Middleware (see process_spider_input()).\n[...]\n7. The Spider processes the Response and returns scraped items and new Requests (to follow) to the Engine, passing through the Spider Middleware (see process_spider_output()).\n[...]\nThe Downloader is responsible for fetching web pages and feeding them to the engine which, in turn, feeds them to the spiders.\n[...]\n### Downloader middlewares\n[...]\nDownloader middlewares are specific hooks that sit between the Engine and the Downloader and process requests when they pass from the Engine to the Downloader, and responses that pass from Downloader to the Engine.\n[...]\nUse a Downloader middleware if you need to do one of the following:\n[...]\n- process a request just before it is sent to the Downloader (i.e. right before Scrapy sends the request to the website);\n- change received response before passing it to a spider;\n- send a new Request instead of passing received response to a spider;\n- pass response to a spider without fetching a web page;\n- silently drop some requests.\n[...]\n### Spider middlewares\n[...]\nSpider middlewares are specific hooks that sit between the Engine and the Spiders and are able to process spider input (responses) and output (items and requests).\n[...]\nUse a Spider middleware if you need to\n[...]\n- post-process output of spider callbacks - change/add/remove requests or items;\n- post-process start requests or items;\n- handle spider exceptions;\n- call errback instead of callback for some of the requests based on response content.\u003c/content\u003e\n\u003c/result\u003e\n\n\u003cresult id=\"2\"\u003e\n\u003ctitle\u003eScrapy Tutorial: Architecture \u0026 Best Practices for 2026\u003c/title\u003e\n\u003curl\u003ehttps://groupbwt.com/blog/scrapy-tutorial/\u003c/url\u003e\n\u003ccontent\u003eUnder the hood, Scrapy is event-driven and non-blocking — built on asyncio. Once a spider fires up, the engine takes over the full request lifecycle. It grabs URLs from the scheduler, pushes them through the downloader, hands responses off to spider callbacks, and routes whatever structured data comes out into the pipeline chain.\n[...]\nThe Scrapy architecture has five core components connected through an asynchronous event loop. Knowing how they interact matters for debugging performance issues and extending the framework.\n[...]\n’ll have dozens going at once, each pointed at a different domain.\n[...]\nHTTP requests go out, responses come back — that’s the downloader’s job. It obeys your Scrapy concurrency settings:&#96;CONCURRENT_REQUESTS&#96; sits at 16 by default in the framework, while&#96;CONCURRENT_REQUESTS_PER_DOMAIN&#96; is 8 at the framework level, but only 1 in the project template. Download handlers — including the ones behind Scrapy + Playwright — run fully async.\n[...]\nTwo layers here. Scrapy middleware splits into downloader middleware (sitting between engine and downloader) and spider middleware (between engine and spiders). Outbound, it handles Scrapy with proxy rotation across IP pools, manages cookies and sessions, and implements Scrapy anti-bot handling through user-agent rotation and header randomization. Inbound, it applies anti-scraping countermeasures — catching blocks, triggering retries, and filtering bad responses before they reach the spider. If you want to understand how sites fight back against scrapers, we wrote a separate piece on how to prevent web scraping.\n[...]\n- RetryMiddleware — retry logic with configurable backoff\n[...]\n- HttpProxyMiddleware — proxy rotation across request pools\n[...]\n- UserAgentMiddleware — user-agent string management\n[...]\n- HttpCompressionMiddleware — response decompression with protection against decompression bombs, patched in a recent security release per the release notes for CVE-2025-6176\u003c/content\u003e\n\u003c/result\u003e\n\n\u003cresult id=\"3\"\u003e\n\u003ctitle\u003eArchitecture overview — Scrapy 2.16.0 documentation\u003c/title\u003e\n\u003curl\u003ehttps://docs.scrapy.org/en/master/topics/architecture.html\u003c/url\u003e\n\u003ccontent\u003eis controlled by the execution engine, and\n[...]\nlike this:\n[...]\nThe Engine sends the Requests to the Downloader, passing through the Downloader Middlewares (see process_request()).\n[...]\nOnce the page finishes downloading the Downloader generates a Response (with that page) and sends it to the Engine, passing through the Downloader Middlewares (see process_response()).\n[...]\nThe Engine receives the Response from the Downloader and sends it to the Spider for processing, passing through the Spider Middleware (see process_spider_input()).\n[...]\nThe Spider processes the Response and returns scraped items and new Requests (to follow) to the Engine, passing through the Spider Middleware (see process_spider_output()).\n[...]\nThe Downloader is responsible for fetching web pages and feeding them to the engine which, in turn, feeds them to the spiders.\n[...]\nDownloader middlewares are specific hooks that sit between the Engine and the Downloader and process requests when they pass from the Engine to the Downloader, and responses that pass from Downloader to the Engine.\n[...]\nUse a Downloader middleware if you need to do one of the following:\n[...]\nprocess a request just before it is sent to the Downloader (i.e. right before Scrapy sends the request to the website);\n[...]\nchange received response before passing it to a spider;\n[...]\nsend a new Request instead of passing received response to a spider;\n[...]\npass response to a spider without fetching a web page;\n[...]\nsome requests.\n[...]\n### Spider middlewares\n[...]\nSpider middlewares are specific hooks that sit between the Engine and the Spiders and are able to process spider input (responses) and output (items and requests).\n[...]\nUse a Spider middleware if you need to\n[...]\npost-process output of spider callbacks - change/add/remove requests or items;\n[...]\npost-process start requests or items;\n[...]\nhandle spider exceptions;\n[...]\ncall errback instead of callback for some of the requests based on response content.\u003c/content\u003e\n\u003c/result\u003e\n\n\u003cresult id=\"4\"\u003e\n\u003ctitle\u003eDownloader Middleware — Scrapy 2.16.0 documentation\u003c/title\u003e\n\u003curl\u003ehttps://docs.scrapy.org/en/latest/topics/downloader-middleware.html\u003c/url\u003e\n\u003ccontent\u003eThe downloader middleware is a framework of hooks into Scrapy’s request/response processing. It’s a light, low-level system for globally altering Scrapy’s requests and responses.\n[...]\nTo activate a downloader middleware component, add it to the DOWNLOADER_MIDDLEWARES setting, which is a dict whose keys are the middleware class paths and their values are the middleware orders.\n[...]\nThe DOWNLOADER_MIDDLEWARES setting is merged with the DOWNLOADER_MIDDLEWARES_BASE setting defined in Scrapy (and not meant to be overridden) and then sorted by order to get the final sorted list of enabled middlewares: the first middleware is the one closer to the engine and the last is the one closer to the downloader. In other words, the process_request() method of each middleware will be invoked in increasing middleware order (100, 200, 300, …) and the process_response() method of each middleware will be invoked in decreasing order.\n[...]\nTo decide which order to assign to your middleware see the DOWNLOADER_MIDDLEWARES_BASE setting and pick a value according to where you want to insert the middleware. The order does matter because each middleware performs a different action and your middleware could depend on some previous (or subsequent) middleware being applied.\n[...]\nIf you want to disable a built-in middleware (the ones defined in DOWNLOADER_MIDDLEWARES_BASE and enabled by default) you must define it in your project’s DOWNLOADER_MIDDLEWARES setting and assign &#96;None&#96; as its\n[...]\n. For example, if you\n[...]\nEach downloader middleware is a component that defines one or more of these methods:\n[...]\nThis page describes all downloader middleware components that come with Scrapy. For information on how to use them and how to write your own downloader middleware, see the downloader middleware usage guide.\n[...]\nThis middleware provides low-level cache to all HTTP requests and responses. It has to be combined with a cache storage backend as well as a cache policy.\u003c/content\u003e\n\u003c/result\u003e\n\n\u003cresult id=\"5\"\u003e\n\u003ctitle\u003eWhat is the difference between Scrapy's spider middleware and downloader middleware? [closed]\u003c/title\u003e\n\u003curl\u003ehttps://stackoverflow.com/questions/17872753/what-is-the-difference-between-scrapys-spider-middleware-and-downloader-middlew\u003c/url\u003e\n\u003ccontent\u003eWhile they have almost identical interfaces, they serve different purposes:\n[...]\n* Downloader middlewares modify requests and responses or generate requests in response to responses. They don't directly interact with spiders. Some examples are middlewares that implement cookies, caching, proxies, redirects, setting user-agent headers, etc. They just add functionality to the downloader system.\n[...]\n* Spider middlewares modify things that pass in and out of spiders, like requests, items, exceptions, and&#96;start\\_requests&#96;. They do share some basic functionality with downloader middlewares, but they can't generate requests in response to responses. They stand between the spiders and the downloader. One example is filtering out responses with bad HTTP status codes.\n[...]\nSome middlewares can function as either a downloader middleware or a spider middleware, but they're often trivial and will be forced into one category or the other once you add more complex functionality.\u003c/content\u003e\n\u003c/result\u003e",

_**Agent (default)**_

Created a reference guide at **`docs/building-a-good-crawler.md`** for Zcrawler and future work.

### What’s in the guide

1. **Mental model** — Closed-loop pipeline; frontier + dedup as core state  
2. **Architecture** — Components, data flow diagram, scaling paths (single → coordinator → host-partitioned → Kafka)  
3. **URL frontier** — Mercator-style front/back queues, normalization rules, priority and recrawl  
4. **Politeness & RFC 9309** — Per-host rate limits, robots parsing (500 KiB, 24h cache, 4xx vs 5xx), User-Agent  
5. **Deduplication** — URL Bloom + store; SimHash for near-duplicates  
6. **HTTP fetching** — Timeouts, redirects, status handling, JS rendering tradeoffs  
7. **Parsing** — Link extraction, sitemaps, canonical URLs  
8. **Storage** — Per-URL schema, hot/warm/cold tiers  
9. **Scaling** — Throughput estimates, host partitioning, DNS caching  
10. **Ethics & legal** — Scope, ToS, PII, AI crawlers, provenance (non-legal-advice framing)  
11. **Traps & safety** — Depth caps, circuit breakers, security notes  
12. **Observability** — Rotating structured logs and metrics (aligned with your logging preference)  
13. **Implementation ladder** — Prototype → Scrapy → distributed  
14. **Zcrawler checklist** — Production-ready gate before a real crawl  

Sources include [RFC 9309](https://www.rfc-editor.org/rfc/rfc9309.html), Mercator/UbiCrawler-style designs, Common Crawl scale notes, and [Scrapy’s architecture](https://docs.scrapy.org/en/stable/topics/architecture.html) for a practical Python path.

When you start implementing Zcrawler, use **§14 Zcrawler checklist** as the definition of done for a first production crawl. If you want, we can next turn that into a project structure (Scrapy vs custom `httpx`) or a `ROADMAP.md` tied to the guide.

---

_**User**_

create a crawler or find a good crawler we can use online and make it so it pulls all the businesses in valparaiso, indiana on a list with the phone number, website, location, and the type of business for each business in an easy to read manner. it should also organize the list by closeness to 410 glendale blvd, valparaiso, indiana 46383.

---

_**Agent (default)**_

Built it and ran it end-to-end.

I created a crawler script at `scripts/valpo_business_crawler.py` that uses free OpenStreetMap sources (Nominatim + Overpass) to pull Valparaiso businesses, then sorts them by distance to `410 Glendale Blvd, Valparaiso, Indiana 46383`.

### Generated outputs
- Easy-to-read list: `output/valparaiso_businesses.md`
- Spreadsheet-friendly: `output/valparaiso_businesses.csv`
- Raw structured data: `output/valparaiso_businesses.json`

### Result
- Total businesses found: **523**
- Sorted by closest first (distance in miles)
- Includes:
  - business name
  - phone number
  - website
  - location/address
  - business type

### Re-run anytime
```bash
python scripts/valpo_business_crawler.py
```

If you want, I can next add:
1) filtering by only records that have phone + website,  
2) category groupings (restaurants, medical, retail, etc.), and  
3) a “top N closest” version for quick outreach.

---

_**User**_

research improvements we can do to the crawler especially to get more and better results and add them

---

_**Agent (default)**_

I’m going to upgrade the crawler itself with researched quality improvements (better source coverage, cleaner filtering, richer enrichment, and improved ranking), then regenerate the output so you get a higher-quality list immediately.

_**Agent (default)**_

<tool-use data-tool-type="unknown" data-tool-name="web_search">
<details>
<summary>Tool use: **web_search**</summary>


Parameters:

```json
{
  "searchTerm": "Overpass query filter non-commercial amenities park school to get business listings"
}
```
Result:

```json
{
  "isFinal": true,
  "references": [
    {
      "chunk": "Links:\n1. [A Practical Guide to OpenStreetMap's Overpass API Query Language](https://riccardoscott1.github.io/articles/Geospatial-Series/OpenStreetMap-Data)\n2. [Loading Data from OpenStreetMap with Python and the Overpass API - njanakiev](https://janakiev.com/blog/openstreetmap-with-python-and-overpass-api/)\n3. [Overpass API/Overpass QL - OpenStreetMap Wiki](https://wiki.openstreetmap.org/wiki/Overpass_API/Overpass_QL)\n4. [Overpass API/Overpass API by Example - OpenStreetMap Wiki](http://wiki.osm.org/wiki/Overpass_API_query_example)\n5. [◶ overpass turbo](http://overpass-turbo.eu/?Q=%5Bout%3Ajson%5D%3B%28node%5B%22amenity%3A2020-2026%22%5D%3Bway%5B%22amenity%3A2020-2026%22%5D%3B%3E%3Brel%5B%22amenity%3A2020-2026%22%5D%3B%29%3Bout+meta%3B\u0026R=)\n\nSynthesis:\nTo get business listings while excluding non-commercial amenities like schools and parks, use the Overpass API to filter by tag and use regular expressions to negate the unwanted amenity types [1][2][3].\n\nUse the following query structure in Overpass Turbo [4][5]:\n\n[out:json][timeout:25];\n(\n nwr[\"amenity\"~\"^(?!school|park|playground|garden|bench).*$\"]({{bbox}});\n nwr[\"shop\"]({{bbox}});\n nwr[\"office\"]({{bbox}}););\nout center;\n\nThis query retrieves nodes, ways, and relations that have amenity tags (excluding schools, parks, and other non-commercial facilities) as well as objects explicitly tagged as shops or offices [1][6][3][7]. You can adjust the regex &#96;^(?!...)&#96; list to include any other categories you wish to filter out [8][7].\n\nHighlights:\n\u003cresult id=\"1\"\u003e\n\u003ctitle\u003eA Practical Guide to OpenStreetMap's Overpass API Query Language\u003c/title\u003e\n\u003curl\u003ehttps://riccardoscott1.github.io/articles/Geospatial-Series/OpenStreetMap-Data\u003c/url\u003e\n\u003ccontent\u003eThe Overpass API is a powerful and efficient way to extract targeted geographic data from OpenStreetMap (OSM). Instead of downloading large and complex OSM datasets, the Overpass API lets you query and retrieve only the specific data they need, such as buildings, roads, or amenities, based on location or tags.\n[...]\nAt its core is the Overpass Query Language (Overpass QL)— a flexible scripting language that makes it easy to filter, search, and process OSM data. Whether you’re looking for bus stops in London, rivers in Germany, or cycling infrastructure in Amsterdam, Overpass QL helps you access precise OSM data for your project.\n[...]\n- Output Options: Controlling what data is returned and in what format.\n- Filters: Selecting elements based on location, tags, and attributes.\n- Data Types: Nodes (points), Ways (lines/polygons), and Relations (grouped elements).\n[...]\n1. An output statement to format the results.\n2. Filters to refine results based on tags or properties.\n3. A query to select elements (nodes, ways, or relations).\n4. A bounding box or area filter (optional but recommended for efficiency).\n[...]\namenity=cafe&#96;)\n[...]\nThe query above combines both area filters and bounding boxes, but it’s important to know how they differ.&#96;area[name=\"Paris\"]&#96; uses OSM’s administrative boundaries (like city limits), but as we saw it’s prone to data entry errors.&#96;(48.52,1.73,49.22,3.00)&#96; is a coordinate-based bounding box. Overpass will return results that match both conditions — i.e., cafés within the named Paris area and within the bounding box.\n[...]\ncurl -X GET \"https://\n[...]\n.de/api/interpreter\n[...]\nimport requests\n \nquery = \"\"\"\n[...]\nout:json];\n[...]\n.searchArea);\n[...]\nThe Overpass API Query Language is a versatile and efficient tool for extracting custom OSM data. Whether you’re looking for specific amenities, analysing transportation networks, or tracking changes in geographic data, Overpass QL provides a powerful way to interact with OpenStreetMap.\u003c/content\u003e\n\u003c/result\u003e\n\n\u003cresult id=\"2\"\u003e\n\u003ctitle\u003eLoading Data from OpenStreetMap with Python and the Overpass API - njanakiev\u003c/title\u003e\n\u003curl\u003ehttps://janakiev.com/blog/openstreetmap-with-python-and-overpass-api/\u003c/url\u003e\n\u003ccontent\u003eNow we’ll take a look how to load data from OSM. The Overpass API uses a custom query language to define the queries. It takes some time getting used to, but luckily there is Overpass Turbo by Martin Raifer which comes in handy to interactively evaluate our queries directly in the browser. Let’s say you want to query nodes for cafes, then your query looks like\n[...]\nwhere each statement in the query source code ends with a semicolon. This query starts by specifying the component we want to query, which is in this case a node. We are applying a filter by tag on our query which looks for all the nodes where the key-value pair is&#96;\"amenity\"=\"cafe\"&#96;. There are different options to filter by tag which can be found in the documentation. There is a variety of tags to choose from, one common key is amenity which covers various community facilities like cafe, restaurant or just a bench. To have an overview of most of the other possible tags in OSM take a look at the OSM Map Features or taginfo.\n[...]\nAnother way to filter queries is by area which can be specified like&#96;area[\"ISO3166-1\"=\"GB\"][admin_level=2];&#96; which gives us the area for Great Britain. We can use this now as a filter for the query by adding&#96;(area)&#96; to our statement as in\n[...]\ndata with the\n[...]\ncommand line tools curl or wget\n[...]\nIn order to\n[...]\naccess one of the Overpass API endpoints, where\n[...]\none we will look go by the format&#96;http://overpass-api.de/api/interpreter?data=query\n[...]\ngarten query now with Python by\n[...]\nin order to\n[...]\nAnother way to access the Overpass API with Python is by\n[...]\nthe overpy package as a wrapper. Here you can\n[...]\nhow we can translate the previous example with the\n[...]\nr = api.query(\"\"\"\narea[\"ISO3166-1\"=\"DE\"][admin_level=2];\n(node[\"amenity\"=\"biergarten\"](area);\n way[\"amenity\"=\"biergarten\"](area);\n rel[\"amenity\"=\"biergarten\"](area);\n);\nout center;\n\"\"\")\u003c/content\u003e\n\u003c/result\u003e\n\n\u003cresult id=\"3\"\u003e\n\u003ctitle\u003eOverpass API/Overpass QL - OpenStreetMap Wiki\u003c/title\u003e\n\u003curl\u003ehttps://wiki.openstreetmap.org/wiki/Overpass_API/Overpass_QL\u003c/url\u003e\n\u003ccontent\u003edisjunctions (truth tests), as\n[...]\n.\n- Standalone queries: These are complete statements on their own. They may perform functions such as querying the Overpass\n[...]\nto create a set; manipulating the contents of an existing set; or sending the end results of a query to an output location. Standalone queries are themselves comprised of smaller Overpass QL language components, such as evaluators, filters, and operators.\n[...]\n### Query for areas (is_in)\n[...]\nis_in cannot be directly used with any of the Overpass QL filters. For filtering the is_in result, a further query is needed (see below): the [square brackets] shown below, indicate optional parts and are not part of the syntax to type.\n[...]\n### The Query Statement\n[...]\nThe most important statement is the query statement. This is not a single statement but rather consists of one of the type specifiers node, way, relation (or shorthand rel), derived, area, or nwr (shorthand for \"nodes, ways or relations\") followed by one or more filters. The result set is the set of all elements that match the conditions of all the filters.\n[...]\n### The Query Filter\n[...]\nThe query filter can be added as condition to a query statement. It has an evaluator as argument and it lets pass only those elements for which the expression returns boolean true.\n[...]\n### By tag (has-kv)\n[...]\nThe has-kv filter selects all elements that have or have not a tag with a certain value.\n[...]\nsupports the basic OSM types node, way, and relation as well as the extended type area.\n[...]\n### By input set (.setname)\n[...]\n\"item\" filter selects all\n[...]\ninput set.\n[...]\n### By element id\n[...]\nThe id-query filter selects the\n[...]\nof given type with given id. It supports beside the OSM datatypes node, way, and relation also the type area.\u003c/content\u003e\n\u003c/result\u003e\n\n\u003cresult id=\"4\"\u003e\n\u003ctitle\u003eOverpass API/Overpass API by Example - OpenStreetMap Wiki\u003c/title\u003e\n\u003curl\u003ehttp://wiki.osm.org/wiki/Overpass_API_query_example\u003c/url\u003e\n\u003ccontent\u003eThe Overpass API offers a variety of search possibilities. This is also known as querying. The results of those searches or queries can be displayed directly on a map, but it is also possible to retrieve only the data. On this page we will focus on examples that have to be displayed on a map. This means that all queries can be inserted in the code editor of Overpass turbo. From there the query can be executed and the result will be shown on a map.\n[...]\ntry it yourself in over\n[...]\n#### Highway around schools with inappropriate maxspeed (since 0.7.54)\n[...]\ntry it yourself in overpass-turbo\n[...]\n&#96;&#96;&#96;\n// see: http://blattspinat.com/index.php/posts/tempo-50-vor-schulen\n\n[out:json][timeout:800];\n\n// Get all schools in current bounding box\nnwr[amenity=school]({{bbox}})-\u003e.schools;\n[...]\n// find highway=* around schools with radius 50m, ignoring\n// footway and paths. In addition, highways are relevant if they \n// either\n// (1) have no maxspeed tag at all\n// (2) maxspeed tag indicates a value larger than 30 km/h\n\nway(around.schools:50)[highway][highway!~\"^(footway|path)$\"]\n   (if: (is_number(t[\"maxspeed\"]) \u0026\u0026 t[\"maxspeed\"] \u003e 30) || \n        !is_tag(maxspeed) )-\u003e.streets;\n\n// get schools in 50m distance for all identified relevant streets\nnwr.schools(around.streets:50)-\u003e.matchingSchools;\n\n// return results, schools and streets\n(.matchingSchools; .streets;);\nout geom;\n[...]\ntry it yourself in overpass-\n[...]\ntry it yourself in overpass-turbo\n[...]\ntry it yourself in overpass-\n[...]\ntry it yourself in overpass-\n[...]\noverpass-\n[...]\ntry it yourself in overpass-\n[...]\nin overpass-\n[...]\ntry it yourself in over\n[...]\n### Communities without fire station\n[...]\ntry it yourself in overpass-\n[...]\ntry it yourself in overpass\n[...]\ntry it yourself in overpass-turbo\n[...]\ntry it yourself in overpass-turbo\n[...]\ntry it yourself in overpass-turbo\n[...]\ntry it yourself in overpass-turbo\u003c/content\u003e\n\u003c/result\u003e\n\n\u003cresult id=\"5\"\u003e\n\u003ctitle\u003e◶ overpass turbo\u003c/title\u003e\n\u003curl\u003ehttp://overpass-turbo.eu/?Q=%5Bout%3Ajson%5D%3B%28node%5B%22amenity%3A2020-2026%22%5D%3Bway%5B%22amenity%3A2020-2026%22%5D%3B%3E%3Brel%5B%22amenity%3A2020-2026%22%5D%3B%29%3Bout+meta%3B\u0026R=\u003c/url\u003e\n\u003ccontent\u003e&#96;&#96;&#96;\n[out:json];(node\n[...]\namenity:2020-2026\"];way[\"amenity:2020-2026\"];\u003e;rel[\"amenity:2020-2026\"];);out meta;\n&#96;&#96;&#96;\n[...]\nThis is *overpass turbo*, a web-based data filtering tool for [OpenStreetMap](https://www.openstreetmap.org).\n[...]\nWith overpass turbo you can run [Overpass API](https://wiki.openstreetmap.org/wiki/Overpass_API) queries and analyse the resulting OSM data interactively on a map.\n[...]\n[Wizard](https://wiki.openstreetmap.org/wiki/Overpass_turbo/Wizard) which makes creating queries super easy.\n[...]\nMore information about [overpass turbo](https://wiki.openstreetmap.org/wiki/Overpass_turbo) and how to write [Overpass queries](https://wiki.openstreetmap.org/wiki/Overpass_API/Overpass_QL) can be found in the OSM wiki.\n[...]\n### Overpass Queries\n[...]\nOverpass API allows to query for OSM data by your own search criteria. For this purpose, it has a specifically crafted [query language](https://wiki.openstreetmap.org/wiki/Overpass_API/Overpass_QL).\n[...]\nIn addition to regular Overpass API queries one can use the following handy\n[...]\nin overpass turbo:\n[...]\nQuery Wizard\nThe [wizard](https://wiki.openstreetmap.org/wiki/Overpass_turbo/Wizard) assists you with creating Overpass queries. Here are some usage examples:\nExamples\nDrinking Water\namenity=drinking\\_water and type:node\n(highway=primary or highway=secondary) and type:way\ntourism=hotel\ntourism=museum in Vienna\n\"Drinking Water\" in London\n**\nSorry, this search cannot be understood.\n[...]\nNote that you must use quotation marks with strings containing spaces or special characters and that multiple search filters must be separated by appropriate boolean operators (*and* or *or*). Read the [documentation](https://wiki.openstreetmap.org/wiki/Overpass_turbo/Wizard) for more information.\u003c/content\u003e\n\u003c/result\u003e",
